{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

revision.text:
{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|12=draft}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇|12=draft}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|12=draft}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|12=draft}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂|12=draft}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子|12=draft}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄|12=draft}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安|12=draft}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|12=draft}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|12=draft}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇|12=draft}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化|12=draft}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|12=draft}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉|12=draft}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺|12=draft}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇|12=draft}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂|12=draft}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳|12=draft}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|12=draft}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2019年4月}} {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 蘇我 日向 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[飛鳥時代]] | 生誕 = | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 字：身刺（むさし） | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[斉明天皇|皇極天皇]]→[[孝徳天皇]] | 氏族 = [[蘇我氏]] | 父母 = 父：[[蘇我倉麻呂]] | 兄弟 = [[蘇我倉山田石川麻呂|石川麻呂]]、[[蘇我連子|連子]]、'''日向'''、[[蘇我赤兄|赤兄]]、[[蘇我果安|果安]] | 妻 = | 子 = | 特記事項 = }}
'''蘇我 日向'''（そが の ひむか）は、[[飛鳥時代]]の[[官人]]。[[蘇我倉麻呂]]（雄正）の子。
== 経歴 == 「皇極紀」に拠れば、[[斉明天皇|皇極天皇]]3年（[[644年]]）、日向の異母兄であった[[右大臣]]・[[蘇我倉山田石川麻呂]]の娘と中大兄皇子（後の[[天智天皇]]）が婚約した夜に、その娘と密通した。ただしこの件で日向がなにかしらの咎を受けた記録は無い<ref>日本書紀皇極天皇三年一月一日条</ref><ref>藤氏家伝鎌足伝（九）</ref>。
[[大化]]5年（[[649年]]）、日向は「石川麻呂が中大兄皇子を殺害しようとした」と讒言、軍を率いて石川麻呂を追討し、石川麻呂は自害して果てた。その後石川麻呂の無実が明らかとなり、中大兄皇子は日向を[[筑紫国]]の[[大宰帥]]としたが、世間ではこれを'''隠流し'''（かくしながし、あるいはしのびながし）と評したという<ref>日本書紀大化五年三月「是月」条</ref>。
つまり、表向き栄転の形で実際には左遷という意味だが、左遷か栄転かでこの事件の評価は変わってくる。{{誰範囲|date=2025年2月3日 (月) 08:14 (UTC)|当時の半島情勢からみて筑紫宰は外交上重要な職であること、[[古人大兄皇子]]や[[有間皇子]]の事件と経過が酷似していることからみて、事件の首謀者は中大兄皇子であり、日向はその功で栄転したとする見方は多い。また、前述の密通事件も、石川麻呂と中大兄皇子との縁談を破談にさせ、右大臣の威信を揺るがし、最終的に蘇我氏惣領の地位を得るための行動、と考えることもでき、さらには密通事件の頃は既に中大兄皇子の[[シンパ]]となっており、その指示を受けて行動した、と考える見解もある}}。
のち日向は[[白雉]]5年（[[654年]]）、[[孝徳天皇]]の病気平癒のために般若寺を創建したと伝えられる（[[上宮聖徳法王帝説]]）。この般若寺が、奈良[[般若寺]]と[[般若寺跡|筑紫般若寺]]、片岡般若院（古くは片岡寺・般若寺と称せられていた）のいずれを指しているかはまだわかっていない。
[[Image:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|蘇我氏略系図 [[メディア:Soga faminy tree.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]]
== 考証 == 石川麻呂誣告事件の特色は、「改新政府」の権力分裂として、起こるべくして起こったものである<ref name="#1">倉本一宏『蘇我氏 古代豪族の興亡』（中央公論新社 2015年）</ref>。「改新政府」を構成していたのは、非蘇我系王族、[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]の側近、左右大臣を頂点とするマヘツキミ層という3つの勢力であった<ref name="#1"/>。『[[日本書紀]]』には、事件の前年4月に新しい冠位制が施行され「古き冠」が廃されたが、左右大臣のみは大化前代以来の紫冠を着し続けたという記事が見えるが、これは王権と左右大臣との分裂を象徴していると考えられる<ref name="#1"/>。この記事の翌年3月に左大臣・[[阿倍内麻呂]]が死去し、その直後に右大臣の石川麻呂が葬られ、4月には[[巨勢徳太]]と[[大伴長徳]]に新冠位の大紫冠が授けられて、左右の大臣に任じられている<ref name="#1"/>。また、中大兄皇子は、『日本書紀』が語るような、密告を信用して外戚の石川麻呂を殺してしまい、のちになって彼の無実を知って悲しむというような短絡的な人物ではありえず、石川麻呂の「謀反」の一連の流れも、中大兄皇子が、王権の主導する新政に容易に服そうとしない左右大臣を、左大臣の死をきっかけに一気に粛清しようとした権力闘争の現れ、あるいはそれによって新政に不満を持つマヘツキミ層への示威を行おうとしたと考えられる<ref name="#1"/>。日向によって密告された石川麻呂の罪状が中大兄皇子を害することであったにもかかわらず、石川麻呂が中大兄皇子ではなく孝徳に身の潔白を陳述したいという態度を見せているのは、王権内部における権力の分裂を示していると見ることができ、この事件を主導した人物が中大兄皇子であることを暗示している<ref name="#1"/>。
また、石川麻呂を讒言し、「将」として山田寺を囲んだのが異母弟の日向であったというのは、蘇我氏内部における分裂と抗争を明確に示している。既に大化元年の[[古人大兄皇子]]の「謀反」の首謀者の筆頭に、蘇我氏同族の[[蘇我田口川堀]]が挙げられているように、[[乙巳の変]]の直後から蘇我氏の内部抗争は表面化していたのであるが、ここに至って兄弟の直接対決にまでなったと考えられる<ref name="#1"/>。
さらに、氏族内部の抗争が、王権への「謀反」やその密告という形をとって行われるところに、日本古代氏族の権力基盤の脆弱さを読み取ることができる<ref name="#1"/>{{Efn2|豊臣から江戸への簒奪も密告や陰謀の類いなので、古代に限定する意味が分からない。秀吉＝秀次、家康＝信康間だけでなく、福島・加藤らも含め秀頼・徳川は完全に姻戚関係なので、簒奪はこの時より醜いと言える。}}。蘇我日向が対立する異母兄を倒すためには、その「謀反」を捏造して王権が派遣した追討軍に征伐してもらわねばならず、その結果、自分は石川麻呂の代わりに身の保全と栄達を王権側から約束された<ref name="#1"/>。日向が拝された[[大宰府|筑紫大宰]]は、7世紀には多く皇親が拝されていることから、世の人に「隠流し」と噂されるような職ではなく、破格の昇進であったと考えられるが、彼のその動向は伝えられてはおらず、歴史の闇に葬り去られたことがわかるように、結局は日向を利用した中大兄皇子の方が一枚上手であった<ref name="#1"/>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:そか の ひむか}} [[Category:蘇我氏|ひむか]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の人物]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

[[Image:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|蘇我氏略系図 [[メディア:Soga faminy tree.svg|SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]]]

== 注 == {{Reflist|group="注"}}

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Ishikawamaro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika era|ja|大化|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi era|ja|白雉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|ja|般若寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new Ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|qq=}} → [[:en:Emperor Kōtoku|Emperor Kōtoku]] | clan = [[:en:Soga clan|Soga clan]] | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|qq=}} | siblings = [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Ishikawamaro]], {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Muraji no Renko|qq=}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Akae|qq=}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|qq=}}.
== Biography == According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|qq=}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai no Sochi|qq=}} in [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] (654), Himuka is said to have founded [[:en:Hannya-ji|Hannya-ji]] to pray for the recovery of [[:en:Emperor Kōtoku|Emperor Kōtoku]] from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new Ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Uchimaro|qq=}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kose no Tokuta|qq=}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|qq=}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Furubito no Ōe|qq=}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|qq=}} → [[:en:Emperor Kōtoku|Emperor Kōtoku]] | clan = [[:en:Soga clan|Soga clan]] | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|qq=}} | siblings = [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Ishikawamaro]], {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Muraji no Renko|qq=}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Akae|qq=}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|qq=}}.
== Biography == According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|qq=}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai no Sochi|qq=}} in [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] (654), Himuka is said to have founded [[:en:Hannya-ji|Hannya-ji]] to pray for the recovery of [[:en:Emperor Kōtoku|Emperor Kōtoku]] from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new Ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Uchimaro|qq=}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kose no Tokuta|qq=}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|qq=}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Furubito no Ōe|qq=}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

revision.text:
{{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 斉明天皇}} → [[:en:Emperor Kōtoku|Emperor Kōtoku]] | clan = [[:en:Soga clan|Soga clan]] | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|qq=}} | siblings = [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Ishikawamaro]], {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Muraji no Renko|qq=}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Akae|qq=}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|qq=}}.
== Biography == According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 斉明天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[:en:Minister of the Right|Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai no Sochi|qq=}} in [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] (654), Himuka is said to have founded [[:en:Hannya-ji|Hannya-ji]] to pray for the recovery of [[:en:Emperor Kōtoku|Emperor Kōtoku]] from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new Ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Uchimaro|qq=}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kose no Tokuta|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巨勢徳多}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|qq=}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Furubito no Ōe|qq=}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new Ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Uchimaro|qq=}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kose no Tokuta|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巨勢徳多}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|qq=}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Furubito no Ōe|qq=}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|father={{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|qid=Q11625195}}|relatives={{ill|Hatayasu Soga|qid=Q11625201}}; {{ill|Soga no Akae|qid=Q11625208}}; {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|qid=Q3488899}}; {{ill|Soga no Murajiko|qid=Q11625207}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11625200]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 斉明天皇}} → [[:en:Emperor Kōtoku|Emperor Kōtoku]] | clan = [[:en:Soga clan|Soga clan]] | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|qq=}} | siblings = [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Ishikawamaro]], {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Muraji no Renko|qq=}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Akae|qq=}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|qq=}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|qq=}}.
== Biography == According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 斉明天皇}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the [[:en:Minister of the Right|Minister of the Right]], [[:en:Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the [[:en:Taika (era)|Taika era]] (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai no Sochi|qq=}} in [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the [[:en:Hakuchi (era)|Hakuchi era]] (654), Himuka is said to have founded [[:en:Hannya-ji|Hannya-ji]] to pray for the recovery of [[:en:Emperor Kōtoku|Emperor Kōtoku]] from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new Ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>

Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Furubito no Ōe|qq=}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>
