<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:蘇我日向]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:蘇我日向]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:蘇我日向]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|father={{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|WD=Q11625195}}|relatives={{ill|Hatayasu Soga|WD=Q11625201}}; {{ill|Soga no Akae|WD=Q11625208}}; {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|WD=Q3488899}}; {{ill|Soga no Murajiko|WD=Q11625207}}}} {{Infobox noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 斉明天皇|1=Kōgyoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q235296}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Emperor Kōtoku}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|1=Soga no Kuramaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625195}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|en|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|WD=Q3488899|lt=Soga no Ishikawamaro}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Muraji no Renko|1=Soga no Murajiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625207}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Akae|1=Soga no Akae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625208}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|1=Hatayasu Soga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625201}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|1=Soga no Kuramaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625195}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 斉明天皇|1=Kōgyoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q235296}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, {{ill|Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|en|Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|WD=Q3488899|lt=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro}}. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika era}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai no Sochi|qq=}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|en|Hannya-ji|WD=Q1576651|lt=Hannya-ji}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Emperor Kōtoku}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
[[File:Soga faminy tree.svg|thumb|right|337px|Simplified genealogy of the Soga clan]]
== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new Ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Uchimaro|1=Abe no Uchimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657240}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kose no Tokuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巨勢徳多|1=Kose no Tokuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479774}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|1=Ōtomo no Nagatoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432357}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Furubito no Ōe|1=Prince Furuhito-no-Ōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410845}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

[[ja:蘇我日向]]

== Historical Analysis == The false accusations against Ishikawamaro highlight the internal divisions within the "Reform Government" of the Asuka period.<ref name="#1">Kuramoto Kazunobu, ''Soga Clan: The Rise and Fall of an Ancient Japanese Aristocracy'' (Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2015)</ref> This government consisted of three factions: the non-Soga royal family, Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's allies, and the "Mahitsukimi" aristocracy led by the Left and Right Ministers.<ref name="#1"/> The introduction of a new Ranking system in 648, which excluded the traditional "purple caps" worn by the Left and Right Ministers, symbolized growing tensions between the royal authority and the ministers.<ref name="#1"/>
The death of {{ill|Abe no Uchimaro|ja|阿倍内麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Uchimaro|1=Abe no Uchimaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657240}} (Left Minister) in 649, followed by Ishikawamaro’s forced suicide, signaled a shift in power dynamics. The appointments of {{ill|Kose no Tokuta|ja|巨勢徳太|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kose no Tokuta|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巨勢徳多|1=Kose no Tokuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479774}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|ja|大伴長徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Nagatokuto|1=Ōtomo no Nagatoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432357}} as new ministers wearing "Great Purple Caps" further illustrate the restructuring of authority.<ref name="#1"/>
Himuka's involvement as Ishikawamaro's accuser and military commander underscores internal strife within the Soga clan. The Soga had been fracturing since the coup against {{ill|Prince Furubito no Ōe|ja|古人大兄皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Furubito no Ōe|1=Prince Furuhito-no-Ōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410845}} in 645.<ref name="#1"/> Himuka’s actions likely reflect his attempt to secure power by leveraging royal favor, but his eventual disappearance from historical records suggests that he, too, became a pawn in Crown Prince Naka no Ōe's broader political strategy.<ref name="#1"/>

{{ill|Soga no Akae|WD=Q11625208|1=Soga no Akae}}; {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|WD=Q3488899|1=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro}}; {{ill|Soga no Murajiko|WD=Q11625207|1=Soga no Murajiko}}}} {{Infobox noble | name = Soga no Himuka | image = | image_size = | image_caption = | era = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Courtesy name: Musashi (身刺) | titles = | deities = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Ranks = | master = {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 斉明天皇|1=Empress Kōgyoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q235296}} → {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Emperor Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}} | clan = {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} | parents = Father: {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|1=Soga no Kuramaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625195}} | siblings = {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|en|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|WD=Q3488899|lt=Soga no Ishikawamaro|1=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro}}, {{ill|Soga no Muraji no Renko|ja|蘇我連子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Muraji no Renko|1=Soga no Murajiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625207}}, '''Himuka''', {{ill|Soga no Akae|ja|蘇我赤兄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Akae|1=Soga no Akae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625208}}, {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|ja|蘇我果安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kurayamada no Kaoru|1=Hatayasu Soga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625201}} | spouse = | children = | notable_events = }}
'''Soga no Himuka''' (蘇我 日向) was an official of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} and a son of {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|ja|蘇我倉麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kuramaro|1=Soga no Kuramaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625195}}.
== Biography == According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', in the third year of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|ja|皇極天皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Kōgyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 斉明天皇|1=Empress Kōgyoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q235296}}'s reign (644), Himuka engaged in an illicit affair with the daughter of his half-brother, the {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right|1=Minister of the Right}}, {{ill|Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|en|Soga_no_Kurayamada_no_Ishikawa_no_Maro|WD=Q3488899|lt=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|1=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro}}. The incident occurred on the very night she was betrothed to Crown Prince Naka no Ōe (later {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|WD=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}). Despite this, there is no record of Himuka being punished for the act.<ref>Nihon Shoki, Kōgyoku Tennō 3rd Year, 1st Month</ref><ref>Fujishi Kaden: Kamatari Den (9)</ref>
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika era|1=Taika (era)}} (649), Himuka accused Ishikawamaro of plotting to assassinate Crown Prince Naka no Ōe. Leading an army, Himuka pursued and cornered Ishikawamaro, who ultimately committed suicide. Ishikawamaro's innocence was later revealed, and Crown Prince Naka no Ōe appointed Himuka to {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|大宰帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai no Sochi|qq=}} in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}. However, public opinion viewed this appointment as a "hidden exile" (隠流し).<ref>Nihon Shoki, Taika 5th Year, 3rd Month</ref>
This incident can be interpreted as either a demotion disguised as a promotion or a genuine reward, depending on historical perspective. Given the strategic importance of the Dazai post in diplomatic affairs, many historians argue that Himuka's appointment was a reward for his allegiance to the Crown Prince. The earlier affair might also have been part of a calculated effort to undermine Ishikawamaro’s influence, ultimately positioning Himuka favorably within the Soga clan and the reformist government.
In the fifth year of the {{ill|Hakuchi (era)|en|Hakuchi (era)|WD=Q540118|lt=Hakuchi era|1=Hakuchi (era)}} (654), Himuka is said to have founded {{ill|Hannya-ji|en|Hannya-ji|WD=Q1576651|lt=Hannya-ji|1=Hannya-ji}} to pray for the recovery of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|en|Emperor Kōtoku|WD=Q317980|lt=Emperor Kōtoku|1=Emperor Kōtoku}} from illness. However, it remains uncertain whether this refers to the Hannya-ji in Nara, Tsukushi Hannya-ji, or Kataoka Hannya-in.<ref>''Jōgū Shōtoku Hōō Teisetsu''</ref>
